title: Numbers 11 to 20 and Ism al-Tafdeel: Rules and Applications
summary: The session focuses on the rules for numbers 11 to 20 in Arabic, emphasizing how they interact with counted nouns (maʿadūd) and their grammatical cases. The teacher explains special gender matching rules for 11 and 12 (ʿasharan wa-iḥdā ashra), while numbers from 13 onwards follow different patterns. Later, the discussion shifts to ism al-tafdeel (comparative forms), highlighting their structure as ghayr munsarif (lack of tanwīn) and the use of tamyīz (specification) with prepositions like fī or bi-.
key_points: Numbers 11–20: For adad (numbers), 11/12 match maʿadūd's gender; from 13 onwards, the first number opposes the maʿadūd's gender. Examples:
كتابان مرفوعان (maʿdūd has fatha and is singular):
11-12 use ahda ashra/ ithnā ashra; 13–20 combine 'teen' numbers with tens (e.g., thirteen = thalathatūn 'ashratan).
Ism al-tafdeel (superlatives) are formed as afʿal or afwal without tanwīn, e.g., ahsanu (best), atqā (most pious).
Tamyiz clarifies comparison focus: مِن سِنًّا ('in age'), using fatha even with qacīr nouns like san.
Numbers 11–20 retain fatha regardless of context; special forms exist for 'twelve' (ithnayni instead of ithnanashratan in dual contexts).
questions: [00:43:06,179 - 00:44:00,039] Q: Why is tis'aata incorrect? — A: Tis’aat(a) should have fatha due to numbers 11–20 rules; it’s a mistake to apply mā’s usual kasrah.
[00:31:00,279 - 00:32:00,219] Q: When is at-tamyiz used? — A: To specify comparison context (e.g., afṣaḥ minī lisānā for 'more eloquent in tongue')
[00:13:00,259 - 00:14:00,080] Q: How do Quranic examples apply? — A: Twelve months (ithni’ashratan shahrā) show 12 rule; maʿdūd agrees in case and number.
